title: Japanese stab binding — kikko (tortoiseshell) pattern
subtitle: A six-hole stab pattern that produces a hexagonal shell design on the spine
excerpt: The kikko pattern is one of the three classic Japanese stab-binding stitches. Six holes per page-height, a looping thread path, and a finished spine covered in interlocking hexagons named after the tortoiseshell motif common in Edo-period crafts.
sourceNotes: Japanese stab-binding patterns documented in late-Meiji craft manuals and in pattern sheets reproduced in the Internet Archive's collection of Meiji-era bookbinding literature. The kikko pattern is also described in Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), as an oriental binding form.

glossaryTerms.definition: A binding style in which all sheets and covers are stacked flat and sewn together through holes pierced from front to back through the full stack.
Japanese for tortoiseshell. The kikko stab-binding pattern produces interlocking hexagons on the spine, referencing the hexagonal plates of a tortoiseshell.

content.text: The
kikko
pattern is the most visually intricate of the standard
stab-binding

forms. Six holes are pierced in two offset columns per page-height; the thread travels a looping path that leaves hexagons on the spine face. The binding does not open flat (the pages open only to about 120 degrees), but it is very strong and the pattern is worth making as a decorative object in its own right. This version uses six holes per side with a standard A5 text block.
What you need

attrs.heading: Materials for one A5 kikko notebook

items.name: Text paper, 90 to 130 gsm, cut to A5, grain-long
Cover card, 300 gsm, cut to A5 plus 2 mm at head and tail
Bookbinding thread, waxed linen, 4-cord
items.qty: Approximately 6 times the height of the book

attrs.heading: Thread length
attrs.body: The kikko path is long. Cut more thread than you think you need; joining mid-pattern is possible but leaves a knot visible on the spine.

content.text: Piercing the holes

The kikko pattern uses two parallel columns of holes. Column A is set 6 mm in from the spine edge; column B is set 14 mm in. Within each column, holes are spaced at equal intervals. For an A5 text block (210 mm high), place holes at 15, 60, 105, 150, and 195 mm from the head in column A, and at 38, 83, 128, and 173 mm in column B. This gives nine holes in total per side, arranged in a staggered grid that produces the hexagonal overlap.
Collate all text sheets with the two covers, outer faces outward. Align all edges at the spine and head. Clamp or grip the stack firmly.

Make a piercing template from a strip of card the same height as the book. Mark all nine hole positions, noting the two-column offset. Use the template to mark and pierce all nine holes through the full stack with the bookbinding awl. Work on the cutting mat.
Sewing the kikko path

The thread path for the kikko pattern moves through holes in a specific sequence that creates loops at the spine surface. Start at hole A1 (head, column A, front face). The canonical path: exit A1 (front), loop around the spine edge and re-enter A1, exit B1 (front), loop around spine and re-enter B1, exit A2, loop and re-enter A2, exit B2, loop and re-enter B2. Continue down the spine in this alternating A-column / B-column pattern, looping around the spine edge at each hole before moving to the next.
Thread the needle. Do not knot the thread; the first stitch will lock the tail.

Enter at A1 from the back face. Leave a 5 cm tail on the back. Exit at the front, loop over the spine and re-enter A1 from the front again. Exit again. Pull snug: the loop of thread sits around the spine edge at hole A1.
Move diagonally to B1. Enter from the front, exit at the back, loop around the spine edge at B1, re-enter from the back.
Continue down the spine in the same pattern. At A2, enter from the back face; at B2, enter from the front face; the alternating direction is what produces the hexagonal crossing.

At the tail hole, reverse the path and sew back up the spine in the same alternating sequence, filling in the crossings. The return path will pass through each hole a second time, creating the interlocking hexagons.
When back at A1, tie the tail and the working thread together at the back face with a reef knot. Trim both ends to 6 mm.
Finishing and care

Press the closed book under weight for 30 minutes. The kikko binding does not open fully flat; open it only to 120 degrees to avoid stressing the spine holes. Store flat or upright with support on both sides.
Variations

Using a contrasting thread colour for the second pass makes the hexagons visible as two interlocking colours. Hemp thread in a natural colour against linen gives a more rustic result; silk in a bright colour against dark cover card is the formal version.
